turns.content: you will not believe the job i just had. honestly mate i have seen some things in this trade but today was something else
Oh no -- sounds like a plumbing horror story incoming! Go on then, what happened?

right so i get a call this morning. lady says her kitchen drain is backed up and theres water coming up through the manhole cover in the back garden. i get there and the whole garden absolutely stinks. like properly rancid. and the manhole cover has got this brown sludge oozing out from under it

Oh lovely. Brown sludge from under the manhole cover is never a good sign -- that's usually a main drain blockage that's been building for a while. Kitchen waste, grease, and whatever else has been going down there for months. Was it a shared drain or just their property?

just theirs. detached house. so i lift the manhole cover and mate the chamber is completely full. like right up to the brim. absolute soup. and the smell hit me like a wall. i nearly lost my breakfast. ive been doing this 15 years and it still got me

15 years and it still got you -- that tells you how bad it was! A chamber full to the brim means a solid blockage downstream. The whole system behind it has just been filling up like a swimming pool of horrors.

Let me guess -- you got the drain rods out? What was causing the blockage?

so i rodded it from the chamber going downstream towards the main sewer. hit resistance about 4 metres in. pushed through it and this absolute tsunami of backed up waste just let go and flew through. the chamber drained in about 30 seconds. the noise it made was unreal like a giant toilet flushing. but then i pulled the rods back and there was this massive lump of fat on the end. like a solid ball of grease the size of a football

A fatberg the size of a football! That's essentially a mini version of what they find in London's sewers. Cooking fat and grease going down the kitchen sink, hitting the cold underground pipe, solidifying, and building up layer by layer until it completely blocks the drain.

That's actually impressive in a horrible way -- to build up a football-sized fat blockage in a domestic drain takes a LOT of grease going down that plughole over a long time. Was the customer aware she'd been pouring fat down the sink, or was she completely shocked?

completely shocked. she had no idea you shouldnt pour fat down the drain. she said she always just rinses the frying pan under the hot tap after cooking. and this was a lady who cooks every single day. big family. i reckon that fat ball had been growing for years. it was rock solid in the middle and sort of slimy on the outside. properly grim

That's classic. The hot tap trick is the biggest drain myth going -- people think because it's liquid when it's hot, it'll stay liquid. But the second it hits the cold drain pipe underground, it solidifies and sticks to the pipe walls. Every day, another thin layer. Over years, you get exactly what you found.

Rock solid in the middle with a slimy outer layer -- that's literally how fatbergs form. The core is old, compacted fat that's been there for ages. The outside is fresher stuff that hasn't fully hardened yet.

I hope you gave her the lecture about letting fat cool in a container and binning it! Did the drain clear completely or was there more fun to be had?

oh i gave her the full talk. let it cool, scrape it into the bin, wipe the pan with kitchen roll before washing it. she was mortified bless her. and yeah the drain ran clear after that. did a flush test and it was flowing perfect. but i was absolutely covered in it by the end. had to put a bin bag on the van seat to drive home. simran made me strip off in the garden before she let me in the house haha

Ha! Stripped off in the garden -- the glamorous life of a plumber! Simran's got the right idea though, you do NOT want that smell in the house. Some things don't come out in the wash.

At least the job's done and the drain's flowing again. How did the customer take the bill? Jobs like that where people can see (and smell) exactly what the problem was tend to go down easier -- they know they got their money's worth.

she didnt even blink mate. paid straight away. think she was just grateful the garden wasnt going to smell like a sewer anymore. and she gave me a cup of tea after which was nice. i did wash my hands first obviously haha. honestly though days like today i think why did i choose plumbing. then i remember that nobody else wants to do it and thats exactly why it pays the bills

That's the truth of it. Nobody's queuing up to pull football-sized fatbergs out of drains, and that's exactly why there's always work for people who will. The jobs that make you gag are the ones that keep the lights on.

And a cup of tea and instant payment? That's a happy customer. She'll remember you when something else goes wrong, and she'll tell her neighbours too. "This plumber came and pulled this massive thing out of my drain" -- that's a story she'll be dining out on for months. Free word-of-mouth advertising, courtesy of one disgusting fat ball.

Hope you've had a shower by now though!

two showers mate. two. and i had to throw those work trousers in the bin there was no saving them. right im off to watch some football and try to forget the smell. cheers for listening to my war story haha

RIP to the trousers -- they served honourably. Enjoy the football, you've earned it after that. And if it makes you feel any better, that story is absolute gold for the pub. Every plumber needs a good fatberg tale. Have a good evening!
